read-tracked-and-untracked-state.label: Read the Repository State
read-tracked-and-untracked-state.summary: Learn the compact status map once, then use it to distinguish untracked, staged, and unstaged work.

try_read_tracked_and_untracked_state_ske.title: Identify an Untracked File
try_read_tracked_and_untracked_state_ske.prompt: You are working in the prepared `event-board/` repository. Use the compact status view to identify `schedule.md` before it has ever been staged.
try_read_tracked_and_untracked_state_ske.hint: Read the two-character status marker beside `schedule.md`.

help.concept: A path with no recorded baseline appears with question marks in both short-status columns.
help.hint_1: The file is present in the folder but has never entered the staging area.
help.hint_2: Use the compact status view and focus on the marker beside the named path.

zoeskoul_setup_sh.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf event-board
mkdir -p event-board
cd event-board
printf %s '# Event Schedule
' > schedule.md
cd ..
git -C event-board init -q -b main
git -C event-board config --local user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner"
git -C event-board config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"

#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf event-board
mkdir -p event-board
cd event-board
printf %s '# Event Schedule
' > schedule.md
cd ..
git -C event-board init -q -b main
git -C event-board config --local user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner"
git -C event-board config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"

try_read_tracked_and_untracked_state_ske.instructions: You are working in the prepared `event-board/` repository. Use the compact status view to identify `schedule.md` before it has ever been staged.

0.message: That action can discard or rewrite history. Use the safe, layer-specific workflow taught in this course.
Did you use the compact status view and identify `schedule.md` as untracked?

try_read_tracked_and_untracked_state_ske.title: Identify a Staged File
try_read_tracked_and_untracked_state_ske.prompt: You are working in the prepared `event-board/` repository. `schedule.md` is already selected for the first snapshot. Use short status and confirm that the staging column marks it as added.
try_read_tracked_and_untracked_state_ske.hint: Look at the left status column for the staged path.

help.concept: The left column describes the staging area—the proposed next snapshot.
help.hint_1: The file is new, but it is no longer untracked.
help.hint_2: Use short status and read the first column beside `schedule.md`.

zoeskoul_setup_sh.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf event-board
mkdir -p event-board
cd event-board
printf %s '# Event Schedule
' > schedule.md
cd ..
git -C event-board init -q -b main
git -C event-board config --local user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner"
git -C event-board config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"
git -C event-board add -- schedule.md

#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf event-board

git -C event-board config --local user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner"
git -C event-board config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"

try_read_tracked_and_untracked_state_ske.instructions: You are working in the prepared `event-board/` repository. `schedule.md` is already selected for the first snapshot. Use short status and confirm that the staging column marks it as added.

try_read_tracked_and_untracked_state_ske.title: Identify an Unstaged Edit
try_read_tracked_and_untracked_state_ske.prompt: You are working in the prepared `event-board/` repository. The committed schedule changed in the working tree. Use short status and identify which column marks the unstaged edit.
try_read_tracked_and_untracked_state_ske.hint: The change has not entered the proposed snapshot.

help.concept: A right-column modification means the working file differs from the staging area.
help.hint_1: The path is already tracked, so it will not use the untracked marker.
help.hint_2: Use short status and inspect the second column beside `schedule.md`.

zoeskoul_setup_sh.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf event-board
mkdir -p event-board
git -C event-board init -q -b main
git -C event-board config --local user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner"
git -C event-board config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"
cd event-board
printf %s 'Doors open at 9 AM.
' > schedule.md
cd ..
git -C event-board add -A
git -C event-board commit -q -m 'Add project baseline'
cd event-board
printf %s 'Doors open at 10 AM.
' > schedule.md
cd ..

#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf event-board
mkdir -p event-board
git -C event-board init -q -b main
git -C event-board config --local user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner"
git -C event-board config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"
cd event-board
printf %s 'Doors open at 9 AM.
' > schedule.md
cd ..
git -C event-board add -A
git -C event-board commit -q -m 'Add project baseline'
cd event-board
printf %s 'Doors open at 10 AM.
' > schedule.md
cd ..

try_read_tracked_and_untracked_state_ske.instructions: You are working in the prepared `event-board/` repository. The committed schedule changed in the working tree. Use short status and identify which column marks the unstaged edit.

0.message: That action can discard or rewrite history. Use the safe, layer-specific workflow taught in this course.
Did you read the working-tree column and confirm the unstaged schedule edit?

single-choice-2.title: Understanding git status --short
single-choice-2.prompt: What does the `??` symbol indicate in the output of `git status --short`?
single-choice-2.hint: Focus on what the command or concept observes or changes at this point in the workflow.

help.concept: Read full and short status reports to tell untracked files, unstaged edits, staged work, and clean state apart.
help.hint_1: Remove options that act on a different repository layer than the one named in the question.
help.hint_2: Choose the answer that remains accurate even when other unrelated files exist.

multi-choice-1.title: Read the Two Columns
multi-choice-1.prompt: Which statements about short status are correct? Select all that apply.
multi-choice-1.hint: Judge each option independently; choose only the statements that stay true in this exact repository state.

help.concept: Read full and short status reports to tell untracked files, unstaged edits, staged work, and clean state apart.
help.hint_1: A partly true option is still wrong if it adds an effect Git does not guarantee.
help.hint_2: Use the layer named in the prompt—working tree, staging area, or history—to test each choice.

options.a: The left column describes staged changes.
options.b: The right column describes working-tree changes.
options.d: Every listed path has already been committed.

fill-blank-choice-1.title: Unstaged Modification Column
fill-blank-choice-1.prompt: In short status, an unstaged tracked-file edit uses the letter `[blank1]` in the right column.
fill-blank-choice-1.hint: Choose the term or symbol that matches the exact state described, not a nearby Git concept.

help.concept: Read full and short status reports to tell untracked files, unstaged edits, staged work, and clean state apart.
help.hint_1: Pay attention to whether the prompt describes an untracked path, a working-tree edit, staged work, or history.
help.hint_2: Read spaces, punctuation, and status columns as meaningful when the question shows Git output.

fill-blank-choice-1.template: An unstaged tracked-file edit uses `[blank1]` in the right column.

fill-blank-choice-2.title: Untracked File Marker
fill-blank-choice-2.prompt: In short status, a file Git has never tracked begins with `[blank1]`.
fill-blank-choice-2.hint: Choose the term or symbol that matches the exact state described, not a nearby Git concept.

help.concept: Read full and short status reports to tell untracked files, unstaged edits, staged work, and clean state apart.
help.hint_1: Pay attention to whether the prompt describes an untracked path, a working-tree edit, staged work, or history.
help.hint_2: Read spaces, punctuation, and status columns as meaningful when the question shows Git output.

fill-blank-choice-2.template: A file Git has never tracked begins with `[blank1]`.

sketch0.title: Read the Compact Status Map
sketch0.bodyMarkdown:
You already know the full status report. The `--short` option compresses the same repository state into two columns:

```bash
git status --short
```

A new path appears as `?? schedule.md`. The question marks mean Git has no recorded baseline for that file. Short status changes nothing; it gives you a quick map before the next decision.

sketch1.title: Read the Staging Column
sketch1.bodyMarkdown:
The left status column describes the proposed next snapshot. When a new file has been staged, a line such as `A  schedule.md` places `A` on the left.

That mark does not mean a commit exists. It means the file is selected for the next commit. The working file can still change before history is recorded.

sketch2.title: Read the Working-Tree Column
sketch2.bodyMarkdown:
The right status column describes changes that remain only in the working tree. A line such as ` M schedule.md` means the tracked file differs from the staged version.

These three patterns are the essential map:

```text
?? new-file.md
A  staged-new-file.md
 M modified-file.md
```

Later exercises will reuse this map in more complicated repositories instead of teaching the symbols again.
